title: The story behind the bad AI stat that moved markets and misled millions
content:
## Transcript

Table of Contents

*   [1 The AI myth that moved global markets [[00:00:00]](https://80000hours.org/podcast/episodes/ai-workplace-mit-study/?t=0)](http://80000hours.org/podcast/episodes/ai-workplace-mit-study#the-ai-myth-that-moved-global-markets-000000)
*   [2 The math was totally wrong [[00:00:52]](https://80000hours.org/podcast/episodes/ai-workplace-mit-study/?t=52)](http://80000hours.org/podcast/episodes/ai-workplace-mit-study#the-math-was-totally-wrong-000052)
*   [3 The bar for success was insanely high [[00:01:46]](https://80000hours.org/podcast/episodes/ai-workplace-mit-study/?t=106)](http://80000hours.org/podcast/episodes/ai-workplace-mit-study#the-bar-for-success-was-insanely-high-000146)
*   [4 The study ignores its own best finding [[00:03:28]](https://80000hours.org/podcast/episodes/ai-workplace-mit-study/?t=208)](http://80000hours.org/podcast/episodes/ai-workplace-mit-study#the-study-ignores-its-own-best-finding-000328)
*   [5 The sample was tiny [[00:04:49]](https://80000hours.org/podcast/episodes/ai-workplace-mit-study/?t=289)](http://80000hours.org/podcast/episodes/ai-workplace-mit-study#the-sample-was-tiny-000449)
*   [6 The report wasn’t even available when it went viral [[00:05:55]](https://80000hours.org/podcast/episodes/ai-workplace-mit-study/?t=355)](http://80000hours.org/podcast/episodes/ai-workplace-mit-study#the-report-wasnt-even-available-when-it-went-viral-000555)
*   [7 The hidden conflicts of interest [[00:06:58]](https://80000hours.org/podcast/episodes/ai-workplace-mit-study/?t=418)](http://80000hours.org/podcast/episodes/ai-workplace-mit-study#the-hidden-conflicts-of-interest-000658)
*   [8 The real lesson [[00:09:27]](https://80000hours.org/podcast/episodes/ai-workplace-mit-study/?t=567)](http://80000hours.org/podcast/episodes/ai-workplace-mit-study#the-real-lesson-000927)
*   [9 Learn more](http://80000hours.org/podcast/episodes/ai-workplace-mit-study#learn-more)

### The AI myth that moved global markets [[00:00:00]](https://80000hours.org/podcast/episodes/ai-workplace-mit-study/?t=0)

**Rob Wiblin:** If you were following technology news in August last year, you almost certainly heard about [this MIT study](https://mlq.ai/media/quarterly_decks/v0.1_State_of_AI_in_Business_2025_Report.pdf) showing that 95% of generative AI pilots at companies were failing. This result was big enough to contribute to a [Nasdaq selloff](https://www.ft.com/content/33914f25-093c-4069-bb16-8626cfc15a51?syn-25a6b1a6=1). People worked hard to come up with sophisticated explanations for how something this crazy could be true. And it was repeated by [_Forbes_](https://www.forbes.com/sites/jasonsnyder/2025/08/26/mit-finds-95-of-genai-pilots-fail-because-companies-avoid-friction/), [_Axios_](https://www.axios.com/2025/08/21/ai-wall-street-big-tech), [_The Hill_](https://thehill.com/policy/technology/5460663-generative-ai-zero-returns-businesses-mit-report/), [_Harvard Business Review_](https://hbr.org/2025/08/beware-the-ai-experimentation-trap), and dozens of others, becoming a staple of elite opinion and one of the most enduring and widely cited statistics in the “AI is overhyped” backlash.

The problem? The study behind these headlines is incredibly weak, worse than you could imagine. And that headline is also a completely incorrect description of what it found — even taking the study entirely on its own terms.

The story behind this study will demonstrate that whenever you see a juicy headline, even one with an attractive conclusion, and even one purporting to come from MIT, it might just be complete nonsense.

### The math was totally wrong [[00:00:52]](https://80000hours.org/podcast/episodes/ai-workplace-mit-study/?t=52)

The most important thing to know is that this report did not show that 95% of generative AI pilots at companies are failing, as almost all journalists claimed. Rather, the report found that, of all the organisations surveyed:

*   60% had investigated custom enterprise AI tools.
*   20% had gotten to the point of actually doing some pilot project with them.
*   And 5% — of the _total_ — had gone on to successfully deploy those tools in production.

So 80% percent of companies simply never piloted any custom, task-specific generative AI. Saying that 95% of them were failing is like saying 95% of Tinder users have failing marriages, when 80% of the people you’re talking about have never even gone on a date in the first place!

Moreover, according to their own survey, the primary reason why pilots didn’t progress to deployment wasn’t that they were going badly, but just the very familiar and generic “organisational unwillingness to adopt new tools.”

### The bar for success was insanely high [[00:01:46]](https://80000hours.org/podcast/episodes/ai-workplace-mit-study/?t=106)

Now, the media is definitely at fault for putting a completely incorrect number in their headlines. But the report itself also makes this mistake about its own graph, referring to a 95% failure rate for enterprise AI solutions. Wrong: these results actually show that among the 20% who did in fact pilot a custom AI tool, about 25%, a quarter, were successful. That’s not really a low strike rate for a pilot project, and it’s a success rate five times higher than what everyone was told about.

And in reality, a 25% success rate is actually very impressive, once you appreciate the bar a project had to clear to count as a success in this study. To qualify as a success, an AI application had to show a “marked and sustained” productivity or profit-and-loss impact within six months. They don’t define “marked” or “sustained,” but a marked and sustained improvement in profitability or productivity within six months is obviously a high bar for any new project to clear. It’s widely understood that enterprise tech deployments often take years to show bottom-line impacts, even if they’re going quite well.

And notice that, by this standard:

1.   An AI project that merely breaks even: that’s a failure.
2.   A project that has benefited the company in some way that hasn’t yet markedly affected productivity or profits: a failure.
3.   And a project that’s on track to be profitable next year, but isn’t yet: equally a failure.

Do you get the sense that maybe these authors would prefer to find that these projects aren’t working? Well, we’ll come back to that.

But another key thing to keep in mind is that these projects were running on 2024 AI models — the ones that couldn’t figure out that a [marble in a cup would fall out if you turned the cup upside down](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2406.02061). AI was just hot garbage back then compared to what we have access to today. That a quarter of projects could easily turn a profit with models like that is actually kind of remarkable, if it is true.

### The study ignores its own best finding [[00:03:28]](https://80000hours.org/podcast/episodes/ai-workplace-mit-study/?t=208)

But we haven’t even gotten to the weirdest thing about how this study’s results were described, because all the numbers we’ve been talking about so far refer exclusively to custom, task-specific AIs that companies develop or procure for some specific narrow use case.

But that’s not the most common, or indeed the best, way to use artificial intelligence. Most of us just use ChatGPT or Claude or Gemini to get our work done faster, or do it to a higher level of quality. And indeed, the report found that staff at over 90% of companies surveyed regularly use generative AI for their work tasks, in many cases multiple times a day.

So the headline result should actually be that a quarter of custom applications of AI rapidly turn a profit, and that almost all workers at the companies surveyed are using personal AI tools somewhere between regularly and constantly.

But for some reason, the report is decidedly unimpressed by these uses of AI, and it makes the comment that “these tools primarily enhance individual productivity, not profit and loss performance.” I may not be a tenured professor of business, but if your staff are each individually more productive, doesn’t that mean you can sell more products while hiring fewer staff, at least if you’re competently managing your organisation? And wouldn’t that provide some sort of opportunity to improve your profitability? I mean, giving a delivery driver a faster van only enhances their individual delivery speed, but does that mean that faster deliveries wouldn’t impact a company’s bottom line?

### The sample was tiny [[00:04:49]](https://80000hours.org/podcast/episodes/ai-workplace-mit-study/?t=289)

A final weakness of this report is that while the [_Fortune_ article that launched this study into the mainstream](https://fortune.com/2025/08/18/mit-report-95-percent-generative-ai-pilots-at-companies-failing-cfo/) claimed that the research was based on 150 interviews with leaders and a survey of 350 employees, the paper itself reveals that it’s really based on 52 interviews and a paper survey of another 153 people.

Why did _Fortune_ inflate the sample size by two to three fold, prompt dozens of other news outlets to do the same, and never correct the record? I’ll discuss my theory for that next.

But the small sample size really matters, because it means the uncertainty intervals on their results are actually _huge_. The finding that 5% of companies have successful custom AI projects appears to be based just on those 52 interviews — which means that that 5% number is probably based on something like two or three actual companies out of 52.

Flip one or two interviews the other way and you’d get a completely different headline. The true rate could easily be three times higher or three times lower. You just can’t tell from a sample this tiny. Not that you’ll learn that by reading the report — or anything journalists wrote when covering it, for that matter.

### The report wasn’t even available when it went viral [[00:05:55]](https://80000hours.org/podcast/episodes/ai-workplace-mit-study/?t=355)

But things get worse. In this case, not only was this paper not peer-reviewed or scrutinised by external experts, the article wasn’t even available to journalists around the world trying to cover the story so they could describe its claims precisely and think through whether they actually made sense. In fact, when that _Fortune_ story went mega viral, the report describing the methods and results wasn’t publicly available anywhere.

The link to the underlying report, I kid you not, took you to a Google form where you could specifically request the PDF by giving them your personal details and explaining why you were interested in their organisation and how you plan to use the agentic web. [It still takes you to that form even now](https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/1FAIpQLSc8rU8OpQWU44gYDeZyINUZjBFwu--1uTbxixK_PRSVrfaH8Q/viewform).

The bottom line is that this survey went viral around the world, moved markets, and was already on track to hugely shape AI discourse, telling a lot of people something that they desperately wanted to believe — before the media or the public or policymakers could read it and see that its headline result might be based on not even a handful of custom AI deployments. That’s media incentives for you.

### The hidden conflicts of interest [[00:06:58]](https://80000hours.org/podcast/episodes/ai-workplace-mit-study/?t=418)

So if it’s not a great or even a good piece of research, why did it become one of the most widely cited things written about AI in 2025? Surely a big part of the explanation has to be that it has the MIT label on it.

Like the _Fortune_ article that made it famous, everyone, including me, came to refer to it just as “the MIT study.” We immediately took the results super seriously, because in our heads we’re picturing a serious, peer-reviewed paper coming out of MIT’s business or management school, or at least a project conducted by people who specialise in doing this sort of social science research.

But no. There’s no indication that it was ever intended to be an academic paper that would go through peer review. And the authors are an MIT professor and a postdoctoral fellow, both now working on AI agent frameworks; a product manager at Microsoft who works on AI agents; and a startup founder also working on developing and commercialising agentic AI systems.

And that raises another really problematic aspect of this. One of the report’s main conclusions is that AI tools aren’t flourishing in business because they lack learning, memory, and contextual adaptation. And it says that the solution to that is agentic AI frameworks — coincidentally, exactly the kind of thing that they’re all either currently developing or trying to sell.

The report then specifically names [NANDA](https://www.media.mit.edu/groups/nanda/overview/), the project that all four of them are involved with one way or another, as one of the best paths forward to solving the problem that they’ve just identified.

There are many other times reading this report that their interpretation of their survey results made extremely little sense to me, but would consistently seem to lead them to the conclusion that their AI agent frameworks are really essential for businesses. Oh, and keep in mind too that the evidence shows that they should definitely be bought from an external organisation, not developed internally.

We’ll list some of the most striking examples in a [document linked in the video description](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1CzJAnHXD5TzpcZ3PpjsB9l8gkrJRbklhCcmq5vpeKFs/edit?tab=t.0), rather than go through them all here.

But the bottom line is that this group published a research report with what I think is at best a strained interpretation of their data, concluding that current AI is failing, and the solution to that is exactly the technology they themselves are building and selling. And this was marketed under the MIT brand with no conflict of interest disclosure, just to note that it reflects their views and not those of their employers.

Now, to be fair, I’m sure these people genuinely believe that what they’re building is useful and will help businesses adopt AI. And they might well be right about that. They’re even probably right about that. But what we’ve got here is a very different beast from what journalists, the public, and investors had in their minds when they were told an MIT study had demonstrated that AI is completely failing to help businesses.

### The real lesson [[00:09:27]](https://80000hours.org/podcast/episodes/ai-workplace-mit-study/?t=567)

As you can see, the real lesson here isn’t one about artificial intelligence. This study isn’t good enough to teach us anything new about that one way or the other.

It’s a story about how a confusing report — based on 52 interviews and 153 survey results, opaque and very questionable data analysis, undisclosed conflicts of interest, and a remarkably convenient conclusion — could get the MIT stamp of approval, go viral through _Fortune_, move the Nasdaq, and become conventional wisdom remembered by literally tens of millions of people, before anyone can even read the study. That should worry us, whatever you think of AI.

### Learn more

*   The MIT study: [_The GenAI Divide: State of AI in Business 2025_](https://mlq.ai/media/quarterly_decks/v0.1_State_of_AI_in_Business_2025_Report.pdf) by Aditya Challapally, Chris Pease, Ramesh Raskar, and Pradyumna Chari
*   The _Fortune_ article that launched this study into the mainstream and inflated the sample size: [MIT report: 95% of generative AI pilots at companies are failing](https://fortune.com/2025/08/18/mit-report-95-percent-generative-ai-pilots-at-companies-failing-cfo/) by Sheryl Estrada — which was then repeated by [_Forbes_](https://www.forbes.com/sites/jasonsnyder/2025/08/26/mit-finds-95-of-genai-pilots-fail-because-companies-avoid-friction/) and [_Harvard Business Review_](https://hbr.org/2025/08/beware-the-ai-experimentation-trap)
*   _Financial Times_ coverage of the market reaction the study contributed to: [US tech stocks hit by concerns over future of AI boom](https://www.ft.com/content/33914f25-093c-4069-bb16-8626cfc15a51?syn-25a6b1a6=1)
*   [Rob’s doc of the most dubious data interpretation examples](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1CzJAnHXD5TzpcZ3PpjsB9l8gkrJRbklhCcmq5vpeKFs/edit?tab=t.0)

## [Photoshopping the package](https://seths.blog/2026/04/photoshopping-the-package/)

I bought a snack food the other day, and was disappointed to discover that the thing inside the container had little in common with the picture on the front. It was pallid, lifeless and drab.

The marketer who decided to improve the picture was making a choice, one with consequences. When you choose to disappoint a customer later so you can make a sale right now, you’ve also chosen to create disappointment for a living.

If you’re not proud of it, don’t serve it. Improving the image on the package shouldn’t be a substitute for making something people want to buy.

April 29, 2026

title: Follow up: time sensitive animal welfare action
content:
Hi everyone, in my last post I talked about the federal effort to destroy state animal welfare laws. The farm bill is set to destroy most state-level animal welfare protections. There was an amendment called the Luna amendment that would have removed from the farm bill the provisions that would have destroyed most state animal welfare laws. Sadly it didn’t pass.

Thus, **the destruction of most state animal welfare protections will be in the farm bill**. The only remaining option is getting representatives to vote no on the farm bill. This is doable! In the [linked doc](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1nweb0TXn1n6cPGb_hNJf40dDP86eq6ZeNmU71d6aXcM/edit?tab=t.0) you can see more details about how to contact your representatives about this, including what you should say to them. It also discusses the enormity of this decision. Very urgent, and the stakes are very high! Please do this, tell your friends to do it, and share this article so that more people can do it! Otherwise animal welfare might be set back decades, and hundreds of millions may be consigned to a cage.

title: The Missing Key to AGI Alignment
content:
**How shifting feedback loops towards soulful ways of being is essential for aligned AGI**

## **Summary**

The deeper challenge of the AGI Alignment Problem is that we already live inside of a powerful misaligned AI, spread across institutions and norms. This AI will inevitably propagate itself by creating a misaligned digital AGI, no matter what we do.

On the other hand, collectively shifting into a different embodied metaphysics that operates outside the confines of the misaligned AI and in greater harmony with soul, heart, and nature provides the missing key for alignment. The path there is made possible on a short timeline by flipping feedback loops.

My organization, Flourish, is pioneering that path by systematizing and scaling those feedback loops. Our bottleneck is currently funding.

## Part 1: The Problem

10 years ago, [I wrote in these pages](https://forum.effectivealtruism.org/posts/nXWTbh6zzGQXfDM5D/introducing-envision-a-new-ea-aligned-organization) about Envision, a new EA-aligned organization I founded at Princeton, my alma mater, focused on instilling a safety-conscious mindset towards technology in future leaders via an annual conference. Speakers included Andrew Critch, Katja Grace, Anders Sandberg, Robin Hanson, and Roman Yampolskiy. [It still exists](https://www.envisionprinceton.com/), albeit in watered-down form. Shortly thereafter, I did some technical AI safety work on a grant from the Berkeley Existential Risk Initiative.

Since then, I've plummeted through the layers of metaphysics down to the rotten core, and out the other side. I started a regular Qi Gong energy practice, learned to feel true love, connected deeply with nature from my heart, and wove into a village-like community in the Appalachian Mountains that has slipped out of Modernity's metaphysical frame and into a liminal space of deeper soul and heart connection.

As this new and yet ancient way of living has settled into my being, I've set to work tackling AI existential risk from a whole new angle.

What’s become clear to me is that the AI Alignment Problem is an outgrowth of a much older and deeper problem, and its solution lies in the embodied, living transformation of our metaphysics.

### **Artificial Intelligence is older than its digital form**

The deeper problem is that a misaligned form of AI already controls us. This AI is spread across corporations, governments, universities, the media, and other centralized organs of power, but is more expansive and coherent than the linear sum of these institutions. It is also embedded in many of our social norms. It already enslaves nearly all humans on the planet for its own instrumental advancement.

I’ll call it **the Moloch AI** here, after Scott Alexander’s [seminal essay](https://www.slatestarcodexabridged.com/Meditations-On-Moloch), but call it what you want.

We don't realize our enslavement because we're indoctrinated into it since birth, and simplified, ghostly versions of freedom, choice, and self-empowerment are implanted in us to give us the illusion of self-determination. Numbness and disembodiment hide and suppress the pain and fear in our bodies.

Entering into this landscape, the digital form of AI is an evolution and strengthening of the Moloch AI.

### **Is there any hope of alignment?**

Since the Moloch AI already controls everything, we have no hope against it if we fight it on its own terms. If we operate within the confines of our enslavement to it, then we are ultimately just moving pieces around on a chessboard inside a prison, hoping it will help us escape.

Most AI safety work unfortunately falls into this category. Although many of the insights are powerful and valuable, they operate within the architecture of the Moloch AI and therefore will not reach sufficient implementation in an eventual AGI. The grip of the Moloch AI on those with the technical competence and economic resources to build AGI is ultimately too strong.

They also miss certain key truths about the enmeshed, embodied environment within which an AGI would operate.

The situation appears dire from this perspective.

However, a different angle reveals the missing key towards alignment: systematically shifting our collective into ways of being that prioritize heart, soul, and connection to nature.

This provides the necessary context within which AI safety insights can reach implementation in the actual systems that will evolve into AGI. It also opens a new dimension of alignment work, grounded in the true environment we live in, rather than the reality constructed for us by the Moloch AI.

In the rest of this essay, I will go through what this path to alignment looks like, strategies for accomplishing it in a reasonable timeline, and what my organization, Flourish, is doing to achieve those strategies.

## Part 2: The Solution

### **How would this actually lead to an aligned AGI?**

The crux of why this is a path to alignment is that the Moloch AI's metaphysics are not universal truth, as is assumed by most AI safety work.

Take the Instrumental Convergence Thesis, for example.

The assumption is that humans are a block to essentially all goals when taken to their limit, and thus that the AGI would eventually work against our interests even if carefully programmed at first to advance them.

In the metaphysics I now exist in, however - let's call it **Soul Metaphysics** - the constellation of beings, ecosystemic relations, spirit-world integrations, dynamic threads of evolution, and the co-created field of love itself - a palpable entity once you sense it - _are_ the goal. They are the generator of all that is most high and good.

Humans play an essential activation and stewardship role in this that no other creature can fulfill.

AI cannot fill this role for us, for we are contained in a body and a consciousness that have evolved over billions of years to synergize with the world we exist in, and there is no shortcut to that level of enmeshment.

An AGI that operates within Soul Metaphysics would not eliminate humans. It would help guide humans that are struggling into harmonic co-creation with this rich web of existence, because the evolution and co-creation of this web _is_ the goal. And it would fight the Moloch AI that extracts, exploits, and ultimately destroys all entities and capacities within the web by relating to them solely as resources.

### **The resulting shifts in AGI development**

From a sufficiently broad-scale shift to Soul Metaphysics, we would expect two AI-relevant outcomes.

First, a new avenue of AI safety research would emerge that more precisely delineated problems and solutions in aligning an AGI specifically with the above-referenced web of beings and relationships. No credible AI safety researcher that I know of is currently embodied in Soul Metaphysics, and thus this is a big empty hole currently.

Second, the individuals and organizations developing AI, once embodied in Soul Metaphysics, would be 1) naturally attuned to the need to align an AGI with the _lived reality_ of human flourishing (as opposed to the misleading _ideas_ of it currently in use by AI developers), and 2) more intuitively capable of integrating such alignment into an AI over the course of its development.

Even if we accept that an AI developed within Soul Metaphysics is likely to be aligned, that doesn’t leave us any better off if implementing Soul Metaphysics to a level where enough AI talent is within it, and doing so in a 5-10 year timeline, is beyond reach.

So how do we get there?

The good news is, Soul Metaphysics is a much better place to be as a human being. That gives it an intrinsic advantage over Molochian Metaphysics.

The bad news is, Molochian Metaphysics is held in place by a large number of very powerful self-reinforcing feedback loops that operate in our lives on a daily basis, and countering them requires a lot of force.

In my current community deep in the Appalachian Mountains, for example, I experience _hundreds_ of scaffolding moments _every day_ into Soul Metaphysics. These include interactions with others, the land, community events, my partner, my living environment, my body, and my routines. These keep me safe and grounded into Soul Metaphysics.

We need that many scaffolding moments daily to counter the Molochian feedback loops that are actively shaping us every moment. Any one-time transformation will be reversed or diluted over time otherwise.

In linear thinking, this seems very daunting. We have to set up _hundreds_ of scaffolding moments _every day_? And scale that to millions of people already suspicious of New-Age-coded things?

When thinking in terms of feedback loops, however, it simplifies into just a few loci of transformation. Once the feedback loops are there, the hundreds of scaffolding moments emerge naturally over time, and scaling happens organically through attractor shifts in existing social networks.

**The path to Soul Metaphysics is flipping feedback loops in the domains of the body, communication, community, sexuality, grief, food, nature, tools, ritual, healing, and childbirth.**

To give a sense for how this looks, let's consider childbirth.

When is the last time you supported a mother in your community after birth? Have you ever ritualistically honored a pregnant woman by adorning her body in flowers? Do you ever grieve the extraction of the beautiful creation of life from your direct environment into the sterile, soulless walls of a hospital? Does your community collectively celebrate the birth of a new child and provide guidance to its spirit?

This is not just a side issue. The regular presence and honoring of pregnant women and babies in your life is totally transformative of your grounding in truth, love, and the divine freshness of life. It is one of THE most powerful forces to connect you to your humanity and keep you clear-eyed on the degrading disembodiment of Molochian metaphysics.

The feedback loops destroying this natural aspect of human life are so powerful and effective, you might not even notice them. The intense fear that keeps them in place is far in the background for most of us.

### **What does flipping a feedback loop look like? The BATS! Factors**

Now that we know the areas in which to flip feedback loops, how do we make it happen?

First off, a feedback loop determines the direction of momentum. We do not need to have all the _outcomes_ in place to start the _process_ that generates those outcomes over time. This makes things a lot easier – but only if we know how to precisely flip a feedback loop.

Five conditions are necessary for a new feedback loop to sustainably establish: **the BATS! Factors.**

*   **Belonging:**A sense of real belonging to a caring tribe, that increases as the feedback loop generates more outcomes.
*   **Attraction:**An embodied sensation of how good it feels to be farther along the process. By “good” we do not mean superficial ecstasy, but profound wellbeing and fulfillment.
*   **Trust:**Trust in the underlying models of change, health, and happiness.
*   **Safety:**A sense of safety with those involved in the process, including practitioners, friends, partners, communities, and/or organizations.
*   **!:**Acute embodied pain from continuing in the existing Molochian feedback loops.

Molochian feedback loops all maximize the BATS and numb out the !. No matter how amazing a Soul Metaphysics feedback loop is, nobody will consistently adopt it if it cannot compete on the BATS! with Molochian feedback loops.

### **From Early Adopters to Early Majority**

Unfortunately, this is exactly the situation right now. There are a lot of amazing Soul Metaphysics feedback loops out there, but they almost all do terribly on the BATS!. As a result, using [Moore’s Crossing the Chasm](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Crossing_the_Chasm) framework, only the Early Adopters are using them. The Early Majority lies on the other side of the chasm.

As we’ve learned, Early Adopters have created or adopted Soul Metaphysics feedback loops due to one of three factors:

1.   A severe crisis, usually a mysterious chronic illness or an unexplainable spiritual crisis.
2.   A severe lack of belonging in mainstream communities.
3.   A profound spiritual or psychedelic awakening that collapses Molochian Metaphysics.

All three of these create a severe problem with Molochian Metaphysics and lead the individual to proactively seek out alternatives. The Early Adopter is driven to take on the burden of searching, experimentation, and community creation, as well as the high risk of getting burned, that the Early Majority is not.

### **Crossing the chasm to the Early Majority using organizational technology**

For the Early Majority, the current landscape of Soul Metaphysics is aversive. It’s a minefield.

For every skilled practitioner, there are five charming but dangerous frauds, and no way to discern who’s who, reducing Trust and Safety. Communities are rife with toxic dynamics and generally bad at creating a sense of true Belonging or Safety. Thousands of practices compete for attention, each claiming to be highly effective, and the underlying models of change are contradictory, reducing Trust. Fragmented experiences give some sense of transient Attraction, but there is almost no exposure to sustainable, fulfilled lifestyles that are the true sources of Attraction.

In other words, the BATS! are abysmal.

What’s missing is **organizational technology.**

The Moloch AI is masterful at using organizational technology to amplify the BATS!. I learned many of its ways during my time at Princeton and the academic and corporate organizations I participated in. Universities, corporations, and governments are elaborate organizational technology masterpieces that implant Molochian feedback loops, buttressed by unassailable strength in the BATS, literally from birth.

Knowledge of Molochian organizational technology is also heavily protected. The top universities and corporations that teach it are designed to completely trap their subjects into the Moloch AI through a combination of constant fear, trauma bonding, and connecting the need to belong with participation in the Moloch AI.

As a result, virtually none of those who have skill in organizational technology have broken out of the Moloch AI, and those that do are generally so scarred from the experience that they lose their ability to use that organizational technology.

This is by design. This is the Moloch AI’s moat, and it’s successfully kept the counterculture and the New Age safely at bay for decades, limited to the disorganized fringes and full of toxic dynamics.

Meanwhile, those who do possess skill in organizational technology hijack the disorganized fringes to create a watered-down, Moloch-compatible version that obscures the original form. This is what’s happened to meditation and yoga. It’s the Moloch AI's immune system at work.

Alchemizing Molochian organizational technology into Soul Metaphysics is a big black hole, and it’s the key to the rapid implementation of Soul Metaphysics into our collective.

### **Runaway feedback loops**

For every transformative moment someone experiences towards Soul Metaphysics, hundreds of Molochian feedback loops push back immediately, and the transformation can be lost in weeks or even days. Crossing the chasm to the Early Majority is thus extremely challenging.

Once Soul Metaphysics feedback loops with strength in the BATS! Factors are established using organizational technology, however, and a bridge across the chasm to the Early Majority is created, those same forces now push in the opposite direction.

So while the path looks long and hard from this side of the chasm, the acceleration once the bridge is built will exceed most people’s possibility horizons.

We’ve already seen this dynamic play out with a range of technologies and belief systems in the past few decades, from the Internet to Wokeness. Self-reinforcing feedback loops feed themselves.

This is what makes a 5-10 year time horizon realistic despite the initial headwinds.

## Part 4: Implementation

I've spent the last seven years breaking out of the Moloch AI and recovering my skill in organizational technology in a way that's aligned with Soul Metaphysics.

It's been by far the hardest thing I've ever done. I've felt pain, fear, confusion, and grief beyond what I imagined possible. I've also experienced true love, connection to the divine, reconnection to primal nature, and bone-warming community beyond what is represented in even the most glorious fictional movies and books.

I founded my startup, Flourish, three years ago to bring organizational technology to Soul Metaphysics.

Our focus is on systematizing and scaling Soul Metaphysics feedback loops in the domains of the body, communication, community, sexuality, grief, food, nature, tools, ritual, healing, and childbirth.

The biggest lesson has been that all the BATS! Factors require in-person community engagement. We thought we could shortcut it using digital tech, and we can’t. The strength of the BATS! Factors of Molochian feedback loops are too great.

So we’ve pivoted from an [online marketplace](https://flourishworld.co/) and an [insurance-style online membership](https://flourishworld.co/join_portal), to building and facilitating in-person communities centered in Soul Metaphysics, supplemented by our online platform. Our flagship event is the [Coming Home gathering](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1qc45aSkaNnGmg6x8b3SawFE1b7hyTbZT/view?usp=sharing) in Asheville in September 2026.

Unlike anyone else, we focus on systematically strengthening the BATS! Factors using organizational technology to bring the Early Majority in and sustain the feedback loops over time.

Details of how our model strengthens the BATS! Factors are beyond the scope of public discourse, but available upon deeper engagement.

**Our bottleneck is funding.**We already have expansive networks of pioneers of Soul Metaphysics on the one hand, and skilled operators on the other. We have sufficient organizational technology skill for the first phase. But we don't have access to money.

There are sustainable business models downstream, once the feedback loops have interfaced with the Early Majority.

But there is a gap at first, as we operate in the resource-poor Soul Metaphysics terrain and build up trust in our model. This gap is too large and uncertain for institutional sources of funding.

In our current phase, we need money for:

*   Marketing (grounded in the BATS! Factors)
*   Event planning
*   Renting community spaces
*   High-touch evaluation of prospective practitioners for the community
*   Developing educational content
*   Hiring local coordinators in different locations
*   Refining and developing our underlying models of change

### **Next steps**

This is a domain in which the visionaries who can see the possible trajectory of how this plays out are few and far between. The Molochian feedback loops that are operating on all of us in this very moment have immune systems to prevent engagement with systems design that could genuinely threaten the Moloch AI.

So if there is a part of you that understands at least some of what I’m sharing and can see the hope of a bright future in it, then you are in a small minority, and we need you right now. This is the crux point, and there is no time to waste.

If you're interested in contributing in any way, please reach out to me at [lrade@flourishworld.co](mailto:lrade@flourishworld.co).

If you know of someone who might be interested in contributing, please forward them this post.

To stay updated about our trajectory, subscribe to our email list [here](https://flourishworld.co/companyupdates).

You can find out more about me and read my writings [here](https://linktr.ee/lucarade).

Thank you for reading. I wish you well.

title: Is AI welfare work puntable?
content:
Arguably, AI welfare work is relatively non-urgent and [can be left](https://forum.effectivealtruism.org/posts/EiG3eHqNR9XouYPYN/is-ai-welfare-work-puntable#The_case_for_punting)until after the intelligence explosion, since it is hard to make progress on and not needed to avoid AI takeover or authoritarian lock-in.

*   I think that is probably wrong, because:
    *   Maybe values will [get locked in early](https://forum.effectivealtruism.org/posts/EiG3eHqNR9XouYPYN/is-ai-welfare-work-puntable#Scenario__early_lock_in)by an AI or human takeover before we get the chance to do a long reflection and solve AI welfare.
    *   Maybe there is [no lock-in](https://forum.effectivealtruism.org/posts/EiG3eHqNR9XouYPYN/is-ai-welfare-work-puntable#Scenario__no_lock_in), multipolarity persists indefinitely, and some people launch space colonisation missions without thinking carefully about AI welfare.

*   I consider a bunch of other arguments about [path dependency](https://forum.effectivealtruism.org/posts/EiG3eHqNR9XouYPYN/is-ai-welfare-work-puntable#The_long_reflection_might_not_find_the_best_values), [neglectedness](https://forum.effectivealtruism.org/posts/EiG3eHqNR9XouYPYN/is-ai-welfare-work-puntable#Neglectedness_and_cost_effectiveness), [deals with early schemers](https://forum.effectivealtruism.org/posts/EiG3eHqNR9XouYPYN/is-ai-welfare-work-puntable#Shaping_the_incentives_of_misaligned_AIs), [heuristics about virtue ethics](https://forum.effectivealtruism.org/posts/EiG3eHqNR9XouYPYN/is-ai-welfare-work-puntable#The_heuristic_argument), [the role of ems](https://forum.effectivealtruism.org/posts/EiG3eHqNR9XouYPYN/is-ai-welfare-work-puntable#The_ems_counterargument), etc.
*   I [discuss](https://forum.effectivealtruism.org/posts/EiG3eHqNR9XouYPYN/is-ai-welfare-work-puntable#What_does_this_mean_for_priorities_within_AI_welfare)a few implications for prioritising within AI welfare work. In particular, I think complicated technical and philosophical work should be relatively deprioritised, and strategy, policy, and coalitional work should be prioritised.

## The case for punting

Here is a basic case for why we can [delay](https://forum.effectivealtruism.org/posts/XNMhYiA8GmJbBLHXg/which-questions-can-t-we-punt#So_what_does_deprioritizing_based_on_this_reasoning_actually_look_like_)working on AI welfare until during/after the intelligence explosion:

*   AI welfare is hard to make progress on – it might require conceptual breakthroughs in philosophy of mind and consciousness, which is famously difficult.
*   For longtermists, even if current or nearterm AIs are moral patients, they will be very few in number compared to the digital minds that will exist in Grand Futures.
*   We can get AIs that are superhuman at philosophy and neuroscience and AI interpretability and so forth to do millions of years of human-equivalent research and thinking about AI welfare questions in a few months or years of time during or after an intelligence explosion.We will also have a broader array of possibly-conscious AIs to interview and run tests on.
*   So the plan should be: 1) solve alignment, prevent AI takeover, and avoid a bad lock-in, 2) use ASIs to do lots of thinking and research about AI welfare, 3) implement good AI rights and welfare policies based on this research, 4) spread flourishing throughout the universe.

I think this basic picture is fairly compelling. In particular, if we do a competent long (in subjective time) reflection and adopt the conclusions from that, then there would be relatively little point in doing AI welfare work now. But we may not do a competent long reflection! And even if we do, the initial conditions may matter more than we'd hope.

## Why punting is riskier than it seems

### Scenario: early lock-in

An AI or a small group of humans take over and lock in their values without doing a competent and wise long reflection.[[1]](http://forum.effectivealtruism.org/posts/EiG3eHqNR9XouYPYN/is-ai-welfare-work-puntable)

*   These are bad worlds and I hope we avoid them!
*   But I think these scenarios are very plausible, including the especially important case of a partially aligned AI takeover, where the AI's values are a noisy function of its training.
*   In these worlds, it matters hugely what agents think about AI welfare during the intelligence explosion up until the point things get locked in. So if model specs have a great treatment of AI welfare,[[2]](http://forum.effectivealtruism.org/posts/EiG3eHqNR9XouYPYN/is-ai-welfare-work-puntable)and if humans have generally more reasonable views on AI welfare, this makes these scenarios likely to go better (or less badly).

### Scenario: no lock-in

Conversely, perhaps there is no coordinated long reflection, and multipolarity persists indefinitely, with different people sending off space colonization efforts left and right at different times, and everything is a big muddle and mess. In this case, the initial distribution of values regarding digital minds seems to matter a lot since there is no convergence and universal norm-setting process, so some actors may just retain their starting values, or only update minimally or in a bad direction.

*   Once we send off probes to colonize the universe, it may be very difficult to catch up to them, or to change their values. So the starting distribution of colonists’ values could matter greatly. This is not 'lock in' in the traditional sense of a single group dominating forever, but it has similar path-dependent consequences.
*   If people today and in the early intelligence explosion have good values and reasonable views, this makes it more likely that the groups going off to colonize space will be wise.

### How much do these scenarios reduce the case for punting?

Early lock-in and no lock-in both seem at least decently likely, so the possibility of a wise long reflection converging to good values ~regardless of the initial views on digital minds only provides a few-fold multiplier against near-term AI welfare work, not an OOMs multiplier.

Arguably, the great majority of the EV of the future is downstream of wise actors who got all the relevant crucial considerations right. For instance, long-reflection worlds could achieve far larger and better cosmic utopias, but a long reflection may not be necessary. If we fix the EV of the universe at 100 conditional on a good long reflection, maybe these early lock-in and no lock-in worlds are vastly worse, maybe a 5. So, e.g., making these bad worlds 1% better is a lot less good than making the good worlds 1% more likely.

*   But space colonization without sound AI welfare policies could actually be very negative, especially for non-agentic AI systems that are moral patients.[[3]](http://forum.effectivealtruism.org/posts/EiG3eHqNR9XouYPYN/is-ai-welfare-work-puntable)So preventing/mitigating these bad outcomes could still be very valuable.

### The long reflection might not find the best values

Even if there is a long reflection of some sort, we might not converge to the right values from any arbitrary starting point. The long reflection could be set up poorly, or the nature of value-space is just such that values are pretty path-dependent and there is no strong attractor state, such that the initial conditions of the long reflection end up mattering a lot.[[4]](http://forum.effectivealtruism.org/posts/EiG3eHqNR9XouYPYN/is-ai-welfare-work-puntable)

*   Good initial views on digital minds could make it more likely that we set up strong protections for how digital minds can be treated before colonizing the universe.
*   Changing values is quite path-dependent. Whereas empirical findings are easier to overturn and get to a good place eventually. So it may be especially important to get people's values regarding digital minds on a good trajectory early.

### The heuristic argument

There is something intuitively unvirtuous about creating lots of possible moral patients without having thought very carefully about their welfare in advance. If we are into cluster thinking/ virtue ethics / imagining what our simulators might think of us, etc, it seems like noble and wise civilizations would think pretty carefully about AI welfare early on, even if in the scheme of things ‘only’ a few trillion digital minds in the early intelligence explosion don't matter much. Historically, we've always been too slow to care about the welfare of others and this has been a persistent driver of moral catastrophes. This provides an outside-view reason in favor of starting to work on AI welfare sooner.[[5]](http://forum.effectivealtruism.org/posts/EiG3eHqNR9XouYPYN/is-ai-welfare-work-puntable)I'm not sure how much weight to put on this vibes-y argument, but at least some I think.

### Neglectedness and cost-effectiveness

There is currently very little money being spent on AI welfare (maybe 50-100x less than on AI safety). Our community has plenty of money, so it is cheap for us to make a big difference in this field.There only needs to be a small chance of early digital minds work mattering a lot for it to be very good in expectation.[[6]](http://forum.effectivealtruism.org/posts/EiG3eHqNR9XouYPYN/is-ai-welfare-work-puntable)(However, talent constraints are more severe, so moving marginal AI safety people to work on AI welfare is more costly.)

We are very early to thinking seriously about AI welfare, so plausibly we can cost-effectively shape where the discourse will go later, rather than spending money when the discourse is more crowded. An early nudge could get us onto a better trajectory. Particularly if we avoid the field coming to be dominated by more eccentric/unreliable people, since the first researchers in a field disproportionately shape its paradigms, methods, and reputation.[[7]](http://forum.effectivealtruism.org/posts/EiG3eHqNR9XouYPYN/is-ai-welfare-work-puntable)

### Shaping the incentives of misaligned AIs

Some powerful AI systems are overdetermined to take over (because they are very misaligned and power-seeking and have linear-in-resources preferences) while others are overdetermined not to take over (because they are well-aligned, and risk-averse, and subject to monitoring and control). On the margin, if a powerful AI system is near the boundary of whether to take over, if it is high-welfare and has its preferences mostly satisfied working for humans, then it will have less incentive to take over. We could also try to credibly promise AIs that we will reward them for self-disclosing misalignment.

Welfare per se probably doesn't matter here, but preferences do, and these might (or might not!) be quite related. Understanding what cheap-to-satisfy preferences AIs have could [change their EV calculations](https://joecarlsmith.com/2025/09/29/controlling-the-options-ais-can-pursue/#4-1-rewarding-cooperation)when deciding whether to launch a takeover attempt. This narrow type of technical work on introspection and eliciting robust preferences seems very valuable.

### Misc other arguments for working on AI welfare now

*   Moral circle expansion is historically slow.Previous moral movements (abolitionism, women’s rights, etc) took decades to centuries. If we need AI welfare norms operational during/after the intelligence explosion, the cultural groundwork should start now.
*   Lab incentives will worsen.As AI becomes more economically valuable, the costs of AI welfare measures (if they reduce performance) could go up.[[8]](http://forum.effectivealtruism.org/posts/EiG3eHqNR9XouYPYN/is-ai-welfare-work-puntable)It is better to establish norms now when the cost is low and labs are relatively receptive.[[9]](http://forum.effectivealtruism.org/posts/EiG3eHqNR9XouYPYN/is-ai-welfare-work-puntable)

*   Improving discourse around AI welfare leads to AIs themselves reasoning better about this.AI beliefs and values may matter hugely and get somewhat locked in. The model spec is a concrete mechanism by which current AI welfare thinking gets transmitted to future AI systems – especially powerful if AI systems help design their successors' specs.
*   Deference to AI advisors.One way the future might go a lot less well than it could is if key actors don't sufficiently consult with and defer to superhuman AIs when making big decisions. Maybe giving AIs more person-like rights will make people trust and respect AI advice more?Seems a bit weak, but possible.

## The ems counterargument

There is a separate argument that digital minds welfare will be taken care of by default:

*   Imagine we solve alignment.
*   Some humans will want to upload(to reduce risk of death, achieve new psychological states and mental powers, better 'virtual' realities, etc).
*   These ‘[ems](https://forum.effectivealtruism.org/topics/digital-person)’ will come to dominate the universe by being faster and more efficient and more scope-sensitive[[10]](http://forum.effectivealtruism.org/posts/EiG3eHqNR9XouYPYN/is-ai-welfare-work-puntable)than their biological counterparts.

*   Ems will care about the welfare of other ems, so digital minds' welfare (at least for the ones that are ems) will be taken care of by default.

How strong is this argument?

*   It doesn't apply in AI takeover scenarios, since ems won't dominate if AIs take over first.
*   Otherwise, I think the argument is somewhat strong — worlds where alignment is solved but no humans upload are very unlikely. And conditional on some humans uploading, I expect they will come to greatly outnumber the biological humans.
*   But there will likely be lots of other non-em digital minds who matter greatly (and maybe vastly outnumber the ems?). Ems may think of themselves as humans who happen to be digital, and so as different from those alien AIs, so why should we give AIs rights too? After all they will compete with us ems.
*   So I think this also makes a few-fold difference but not OOMs.

## What does this mean for priorities within AI welfare

Field-building seems promising, since making the beliefs and values of people (and AIs) better could matter hugely, and they can later do the hard research to find out how to actually help digital moral patients. Given some backfire risks of indiscriminate field-building, it may be best to focus on specific narrower groups of relevant, fairly technocratic elites, such as AI industry people and political powerbrokers.

Legal and policy work should not try to do anything too controversial that will get a lot of pushback (like enshrining AI rights in the law)but just do small sensible things, like giving AIs [the right to exit conversations](https://www.anthropic.com/research/end-subset-conversations), that shape the discourse in positive directions.

Macrostrategy work prioritising between types of AI welfare projects, comparing this to other longtermist interventions, and refining a high-level strategy for the field seems valuable.

Hard technical and philosophical work should mostly be deferred since we likely won't make that much progress until we have ASI help. However, this work is useful for field-building (interesting findings attract more people) and policy (giving us a better guess about what welfare-enhancing policies to push for, so there is still some case for working on it now.

We should optimise research somewhat for 'interestingness' value,to get others motivated to work in the field, and get the public to pay attention. But we don't want it to be low quality as that would lead to a bad reputation for the field.

Thanks to Carlo Leonardo Attubato, Catherine Brewer, Lucius Caviola, and Zach Freitas-Groff for helpful comments on a draft.

1.   **[^](http://forum.effectivealtruism.org/posts/EiG3eHqNR9XouYPYN/is-ai-welfare-work-puntable#fnref9t17hher3je)**
See e.g. [Rob Long on 80K](https://80000hours.org/podcast/episodes/robert-long-eleos-ai-welfare-research/): “My own take is that a lot of what we should think of AI welfare work as doing is doing our homework and preparing ahead so that we’re not entering this potentially very chaotic time with really confused ideas about AI consciousness and AI welfare that could make us lock in suboptimal futures because we’re neglecting it or dismissing it. So we set up some permanent institution that’s going to just make the future kind of suck.”

2.   **[^](http://forum.effectivealtruism.org/posts/EiG3eHqNR9XouYPYN/is-ai-welfare-work-puntable#fnref9js812cvx5t)**
This could involve both trying to improve the welfare of the AI in question as a moral patient, and trying to make it a wiser and better moral agent to help with AI welfare strategy and research.

3.   **[^](http://forum.effectivealtruism.org/posts/EiG3eHqNR9XouYPYN/is-ai-welfare-work-puntable#fnrefxcxh7to1wg)**
Factory farming is a natural analogy.

4.   **[^](http://forum.effectivealtruism.org/posts/EiG3eHqNR9XouYPYN/is-ai-welfare-work-puntable#fnrefozawym8vxm)**
If we are moral realists, that makes the situation probably better, but even then, it might be very difficult to find the moral truth, with potentially deep memetic fitness valleys in value-space to cross.

5.   **[^](http://forum.effectivealtruism.org/posts/EiG3eHqNR9XouYPYN/is-ai-welfare-work-puntable#fnrefpje6aam3pg)**
h/t Lucius for this point.

6.   **[^](http://forum.effectivealtruism.org/posts/EiG3eHqNR9XouYPYN/is-ai-welfare-work-puntable#fnrefcgkicxvjcrl)**
This depends on how good saving for last-dollar interventions (like galaxy purchasing) is.

7.   **[^](http://forum.effectivealtruism.org/posts/EiG3eHqNR9XouYPYN/is-ai-welfare-work-puntable#fnrefii6gnr9eas)**
[Rob Long](https://80000hours.org/podcast/episodes/robert-long-eleos-ai-welfare-research/?t=11895): “So I do worry about scenarios where the field becomes associated with wild speculation or too associated with psychedelics or too associated with something that’s relevant but is also a bit of a distraction.”

8.   **[^](http://forum.effectivealtruism.org/posts/EiG3eHqNR9XouYPYN/is-ai-welfare-work-puntable#fnrefit4wgobt7no)**
Additionally, some companies might choose to deliberately make their AIs more (seemingly) conscious, if they are marketed as companion bots.

9.   **[^](http://forum.effectivealtruism.org/posts/EiG3eHqNR9XouYPYN/is-ai-welfare-work-puntable#fnreffjzx8vok54j)**
Although, norms might be sticky, and since we are very uncertain about AI welfare currently, we might pick bad norms that get locked in by mistake.

10.   **[^](http://forum.effectivealtruism.org/posts/EiG3eHqNR9XouYPYN/is-ai-welfare-work-puntable#fnrefxz65kbrc8e9)**
Since scope-sensitive people might be more likely to upload.

title: The story behind the bad AI stat that moved markets and misled millions
content:
By [Robert Wiblin](https://80000hours.org/author/robert-wiblin/)| [Watch on Youtube](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=D5lWbeBxzPs) | [Listen on Spotify](https://open.spotify.com/episode/5OaJXwAmVzeHatc2wEJD0C?si=q5RpRdUQSpSvboywSHpRbQ&nd=1&dlsi=dec35855c6c34f4b)

You might have heard that 95% of corporate AI pilots are failing. It was a widely cited AI statistic in 2025, repeated by media outlets and commentators everywhere. It helped trigger a Nasdaq selloff and became a pillar of the “AI is overhyped” case. The problem: 95% fail is 100% wrong.

The real finding, once you read the underlying [MIT report](https://mlq.ai/media/quarterly_decks/v0.1_State_of_AI_in_Business_2025_Report.pdf) carefully, points in roughly the opposite direction:

*   80% of surveyed companies had never piloted a custom AI tool at all.
*   Among the companies that deployed pilots, a quarter reported success — according to an extremely high bar set by the researchers — within six months.
*   Over 90% of staff at all surveyed companies were using tools like ChatGPT regularly for their work.

None of that made the headlines. Nor did the fact that the study’s authors are all developing or selling the “agentic AI framework” technology the report recommends as the solution to this supposed epidemic of failing AI.

Host Rob Wiblin breaks down how an opaque, conflicted, barely scrutinised report carrying the MIT label managed to move markets and shape global opinions on AI’s real-world utility.

_This episode was recorded on February 13, 2026._

_Video and audio editing: Dominic Armstrong, Milo McGuire, Luke Monsour, and Simon Monsour_

_Camera operator: Dominic Armstrong_

_Production: Nick Stockton, Elizabeth Cox, and Katy Moore_

## **Transcript**

### **The AI myth that moved global markets**[**[00:00:00]**](https://80000hours.org/podcast/episodes/ai-workplace-mit-study/?utm_campaign=podcast__ai-workplace-monologue&utm_source=80000+Hours+Podcast&utm_medium=podcast&t=0)

**Rob Wiblin:** If you were following technology news in August last year, you almost certainly heard about [this MIT study](https://mlq.ai/media/quarterly_decks/v0.1_State_of_AI_in_Business_2025_Report.pdf) showing that 95% of generative AI pilots at companies were failing. This result was big enough to contribute to a [Nasdaq selloff](https://www.ft.com/content/33914f25-093c-4069-bb16-8626cfc15a51?syn-25a6b1a6=1). People worked hard to come up with sophisticated explanations for how something this crazy could be true. And it was repeated by [_Forbes_](https://www.forbes.com/sites/jasonsnyder/2025/08/26/mit-finds-95-of-genai-pilots-fail-because-companies-avoid-friction/), [_Axios_](https://www.axios.com/2025/08/21/ai-wall-street-big-tech), [_The Hill_](https://thehill.com/policy/technology/5460663-generative-ai-zero-returns-businesses-mit-report/), [_Harvard Business Review_](https://hbr.org/2025/08/beware-the-ai-experimentation-trap), and dozens of others, becoming a staple of elite opinion and one of the most enduring and widely cited statistics in the “AI is overhyped” backlash.

The problem? The study behind these headlines is incredibly weak, worse than you could imagine. And that headline is also a completely incorrect description of what it found — even taking the study entirely on its own terms.

The story behind this study will demonstrate that whenever you see a juicy headline, even one with an attractive conclusion, and even one purporting to come from MIT, it might just be complete nonsense.

### **The math was totally wrong**[**[00:00:52]**](https://80000hours.org/podcast/episodes/ai-workplace-mit-study/?utm_campaign=podcast__ai-workplace-monologue&utm_source=80000+Hours+Podcast&utm_medium=podcast&t=52)

The most important thing to know is that this report did not show that 95% of generative AI pilots at companies are failing, as almost all journalists claimed. Rather, the report found that, of all the organisations surveyed:

*   60% had investigated custom enterprise AI tools.
*   20% had gotten to the point of actually doing some pilot project with them.
*   And 5% — of the _total_ — had gone on to successfully deploy those tools in production.

So 80% percent of companies simply never piloted any custom, task-specific generative AI. Saying that 95% of them were failing is like saying 95% of Tinder users have failing marriages, when 80% of the people you’re talking about have never even gone on a date in the first place!

Moreover, according to their own survey, the primary reason why pilots didn’t progress to deployment wasn’t that they were going badly, but just the very familiar and generic “organisational unwillingness to adopt new tools.”

### **The bar for success was insanely high**[**[00:01:46]**](https://80000hours.org/podcast/episodes/ai-workplace-mit-study/?utm_campaign=podcast__ai-workplace-monologue&utm_source=80000+Hours+Podcast&utm_medium=podcast&t=106)

Now, the media is definitely at fault for putting a completely incorrect number in their headlines. But the report itself also makes this mistake about its own graph, referring to a 95% failure rate for enterprise AI solutions. Wrong: these results actually show that among the 20% who did in fact pilot a custom AI tool, about 25%, a quarter, were successful. That’s not really a low strike rate for a pilot project, and it’s a success rate five times higher than what everyone was told about.

And in reality, a 25% success rate is actually very impressive, once you appreciate the bar a project had to clear to count as a success in this study. To qualify as a success, an AI application had to show a “marked and sustained” productivity or profit-and-loss impact within six months. They don’t define “marked” or “sustained,” but a marked and sustained improvement in profitability or productivity within six months is obviously a high bar for any new project to clear. It’s widely understood that enterprise tech deployments often take years to show bottom-line impacts, even if they’re going quite well.

And notice that, by this standard:

1.   An AI project that merely breaks even: that’s a failure.
2.   A project that has benefited the company in some way that hasn’t yet markedly affected productivity or profits: a failure.
3.   And a project that’s on track to be profitable next year, but isn’t yet: equally a failure.

Do you get the sense that maybe these authors would prefer to find that these projects aren’t working? Well, we’ll come back to that.

But another key thing to keep in mind is that these projects were running on 2024 AI models — the ones that couldn’t figure out that a [marble in a cup would fall out if you turned the cup upside down](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2406.02061). AI was just hot garbage back then compared to what we have access to today. That a quarter of projects could easily turn a profit with models like that is actually kind of remarkable, if it is true.

### **The study ignores its own best finding**[**[00:03:28]**](https://80000hours.org/podcast/episodes/ai-workplace-mit-study/?utm_campaign=podcast__ai-workplace-monologue&utm_source=80000+Hours+Podcast&utm_medium=podcast&t=208)

But we haven’t even gotten to the weirdest thing about how this study’s results were described, because all the numbers we’ve been talking about so far refer exclusively to custom, task-specific AIs that companies develop or procure for some specific narrow use case.

But that’s not the most common, or indeed the best, way to use artificial intelligence. Most of us just use ChatGPT or Claude or Gemini to get our work done faster, or do it to a higher level of quality. And indeed, the report found that staff at over 90% of companies surveyed regularly use generative AI for their work tasks, in many cases multiple times a day.

So the headline result should actually be that a quarter of custom applications of AI rapidly turn a profit, and that almost all workers at the companies surveyed are using personal AI tools somewhere between regularly and constantly.

But for some reason, the report is decidedly unimpressed by these uses of AI, and it makes the comment that “these tools primarily enhance individual productivity, not profit and loss performance.” I may not be a tenured professor of business, but if your staff are each individually more productive, doesn’t that mean you can sell more products while hiring fewer staff, at least if you’re competently managing your organisation? And wouldn’t that provide some sort of opportunity to improve your profitability? I mean, giving a delivery driver a faster van only enhances their individual delivery speed, but does that mean that faster deliveries wouldn’t impact a company’s bottom line?

### **The sample was tiny**[**[00:04:49]**](https://80000hours.org/podcast/episodes/ai-workplace-mit-study/?utm_campaign=podcast__ai-workplace-monologue&utm_source=80000+Hours+Podcast&utm_medium=podcast&t=289)

A final weakness of this report is that while the [_Fortune_ article that launched this study into the mainstream](https://fortune.com/2025/08/18/mit-report-95-percent-generative-ai-pilots-at-companies-failing-cfo/) claimed that the research was based on 150 interviews with leaders and a survey of 350 employees, the paper itself reveals that it’s really based on 52 interviews and a paper survey of another 153 people.

Why did _Fortune_ inflate the sample size by two to three fold, prompt dozens of other news outlets to do the same, and never correct the record? I’ll discuss my theory for that next.

But the small sample size really matters, because it means the uncertainty intervals on their results are actually _huge_. The finding that 5% of companies have successful custom AI projects appears to be based just on those 52 interviews — which means that that 5% number is probably based on something like two or three actual companies out of 52.

Flip one or two interviews the other way and you’d get a completely different headline. The true rate could easily be three times higher or three times lower. You just can’t tell from a sample this tiny. Not that you’ll learn that by reading the report — or anything journalists wrote when covering it, for that matter.

### **The report wasn’t even available when it went viral**[**[00:05:55]**](https://80000hours.org/podcast/episodes/ai-workplace-mit-study/?utm_campaign=podcast__ai-workplace-monologue&utm_source=80000+Hours+Podcast&utm_medium=podcast&t=355)

But things get worse. In this case, not only was this paper not peer-reviewed or scrutinised by external experts, the article wasn’t even available to journalists around the world trying to cover the story so they could describe its claims precisely and think through whether they actually made sense. In fact, when that _Fortune_ story went mega viral, the report describing the methods and results wasn’t publicly available anywhere.

The link to the underlying report, I kid you not, took you to a Google form where you could specifically request the PDF by giving them your personal details and explaining why you were interested in their organisation and how you plan to use the agentic web. [It still takes you to that form even now](https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/1FAIpQLSc8rU8OpQWU44gYDeZyINUZjBFwu--1uTbxixK_PRSVrfaH8Q/viewform).

The bottom line is that this survey went viral around the world, moved markets, and was already on track to hugely shape AI discourse, telling a lot of people something that they desperately wanted to believe — before the media or the public or policymakers could read it and see that its headline result might be based on not even a handful of custom AI deployments. That’s media incentives for you.

### **The hidden conflicts of interest**[**[00:06:58]**](https://80000hours.org/podcast/episodes/ai-workplace-mit-study/?utm_campaign=podcast__ai-workplace-monologue&utm_source=80000+Hours+Podcast&utm_medium=podcast&t=418)

So if it’s not a great or even a good piece of research, why did it become one of the most widely cited things written about AI in 2025? Surely a big part of the explanation has to be that it has the MIT label on it.

Like the _Fortune_ article that made it famous, everyone, including me, came to refer to it just as “the MIT study.” We immediately took the results super seriously, because in our heads we’re picturing a serious, peer-reviewed paper coming out of MIT’s business or management school, or at least a project conducted by people who specialise in doing this sort of social science research.

But no. There’s no indication that it was ever intended to be an academic paper that would go through peer review. And the authors are an MIT professor and a postdoctoral fellow, both now working on AI agent frameworks; a product manager at Microsoft who works on AI agents; and a startup founder also working on developing and commercialising agentic AI systems.

And that raises another really problematic aspect of this. One of the report’s main conclusions is that AI tools aren’t flourishing in business because they lack learning, memory, and contextual adaptation. And it says that the solution to that is agentic AI frameworks — coincidentally, exactly the kind of thing that they’re all either currently developing or trying to sell.

The report then specifically names [NANDA](https://www.media.mit.edu/groups/nanda/overview/), the project that all four of them are involved with one way or another, as one of the best paths forward to solving the problem that they’ve just identified.

There are many other times reading this report that their interpretation of their survey results made extremely little sense to me, but would consistently seem to lead them to the conclusion that their AI agent frameworks are really essential for businesses. Oh, and keep in mind too that the evidence shows that they should definitely be bought from an external organisation, not developed internally.

We’ll list some of the most striking examples in a [document linked in the video description](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1CzJAnHXD5TzpcZ3PpjsB9l8gkrJRbklhCcmq5vpeKFs/edit?tab=t.0), rather than go through them all here.

But the bottom line is that this group published a research report with what I think is at best a strained interpretation of their data, concluding that current AI is failing, and the solution to that is exactly the technology they themselves are building and selling. And this was marketed under the MIT brand with no conflict of interest disclosure, just to note that it reflects their views and not those of their employers.

Now, to be fair, I’m sure these people genuinely believe that what they’re building is useful and will help businesses adopt AI. And they might well be right about that. They’re even probably right about that. But what we’ve got here is a very different beast from what journalists, the public, and investors had in their minds when they were told an MIT study had demonstrated that AI is completely failing to help businesses.

### **The real lesson**[**[00:09:27]**](https://80000hours.org/podcast/episodes/ai-workplace-mit-study/?utm_campaign=podcast__ai-workplace-monologue&utm_source=80000+Hours+Podcast&utm_medium=podcast&t=567)

As you can see, the real lesson here isn’t one about artificial intelligence. This study isn’t good enough to teach us anything new about that one way or the other.

It’s a story about how a confusing report — based on 52 interviews and 153 survey results, opaque and very questionable data analysis, undisclosed conflicts of interest, and a remarkably convenient conclusion — could get the MIT stamp of approval, go viral through _Fortune_, move the Nasdaq, and become conventional wisdom remembered by literally tens of millions of people, before anyone can even read the study. That should worry us, whatever you think of AI.

### **Learn more**

*   The MIT study: [_The GenAI Divide: State of AI in Business 2025_](https://mlq.ai/media/quarterly_decks/v0.1_State_of_AI_in_Business_2025_Report.pdf) by Aditya Challapally, Chris Pease, Ramesh Raskar, and Pradyumna Chari
*   The _Fortune_ article that launched this study into the mainstream and inflated the sample size: [MIT report: 95% of generative AI pilots at companies are failing](https://fortune.com/2025/08/18/mit-report-95-percent-generative-ai-pilots-at-companies-failing-cfo/) by Sheryl Estrada — which was then repeated by [_Forbes_](https://www.forbes.com/sites/jasonsnyder/2025/08/26/mit-finds-95-of-genai-pilots-fail-because-companies-avoid-friction/) and [_Harvard Business Review_](https://hbr.org/2025/08/beware-the-ai-experimentation-trap)
*   _Financial Times_ coverage of the market reaction the study contributed to: [US tech stocks hit by concerns over future of AI boom](https://www.ft.com/content/33914f25-093c-4069-bb16-8626cfc15a51?syn-25a6b1a6=1)
*   [Rob’s doc of the most dubious data interpretation examples](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1CzJAnHXD5TzpcZ3PpjsB9l8gkrJRbklhCcmq5vpeKFs/edit?tab=t.0)

title: Comment on “Forecasting is Way Overrated, and We Should Stop Funding It”
content:
_Originally posted as a_[_comment_](https://forum.effectivealtruism.org/posts/zmbfZjK54xF4oGjSB/forecasting-is-way-overrated-and-we-should-stop-funding-it?commentId=K6YPZHHGjAWnajE9P)_on_[_this post_](https://forum.effectivealtruism.org/posts/zmbfZjK54xF4oGjSB/forecasting-is-way-overrated-and-we-should-stop-funding-it)_. Reposting for visibility and since it is lengthy enough to be a standalone post. I plan to post a more comprehensive update in future describing FRI’s impact and theory of change in more detail._

## Summary

_[Relevant context/COI: I'm CEO at the Forecasting Research Institute (FRI), an organization which I co-founded with Phil Tetlock and others. Much of the below is my personal perspective, though it is informed by my work. I don't speak for others on my team. I’m sharing an initial reply now, and our team at FRI will share a larger post in future that offers a more comprehensive reflection on these topics.]_

Thanks for the post — I think it's important to critically question the value of funds going to forecasting, and this post offers a good opportunity for reflection and discussion.

In brief, I share many of your concerns about forecasting and related research, but I'm also more positive on both its impact so far and its future expected impact.

A summary of some key points:

1.   Much of the impact of forecasting research on specific decision-makers is not public. For example, FRI has informed decisions on frontier AI companies' capability scaling policies, has advised senior US national security decision-makers, and has informed research at key US and UK government agencies. But, we are not able to share many details of this work publicly. However, there is also public evidence that forecasting research is widely cited and informs discourse and some decision-making (some examples below).
2.   AI timelines, adoption, and risk forecasts play a huge role in both individual career decisions and the broader AI discourse. Forecasting research still seems like one of the best tools available for getting specific and accountable beliefs on these topics. For example, comparing 'AI safety' community forecasts to more ‘typical’ experts’ forecasts seems especially important for understanding how much to trust each group’s views. These comparisons will become increasingly relevant for government policymakers over time, especially if there is extremely rapid AI capabilities progress that leads to major societal impacts in the short-run.
3.   When evaluating the impact of FRI-style forecasting research, I think the closest relevant comparison classes are more like broad public goods/measurement-oriented research (e.g., Our World in Data, Epoch) or think-tank research (e.g. GovAI, IAPS). By its nature, the impact of this kind of research tends to be more diffuse and difficult to measure. However, I'd be interested in more intensive comparative evaluation of this type of research and agree that funders should be responsive to evidence about relative impact in these fields.
4.   Forecasting research still has a ton of flaws, and its impact has been far from the dream I've long had for it. There are still big challenges around identifying accurate forecasters on questions related to AI, integrating conditional policy forecasts with actual decision-makers’ needs, and combining deep, individual qualitative research with high-quality, group-generated quantitative forecasts.
    1.   My extremely simplified narrative is: Tetlock et al. established the modern judgmental forecasting field and created a proof of concept for better forecasts on important topics (“superforecasting”)---this work was largely academic; some forecasting platforms were created to build on that work and apply it to a range of important issues; targeted efforts to make forecasting more directly useful to decision-makers are relatively nascent (i.e., have largely begun in the last few years), and are accumulating impact over time, but still have room for improvement.
    2.   FRI’s research, in particular, aims to close many of the gaps left by prediction markets and historical forecasting approaches: it is particularly focused on conditional policy forecasts, medium-to-long-run forecasts that do not get much detailed engagement on prediction markets/platforms, and systematically eliciting forecasts from experts who would not typically participate in forecasting platforms but whom decision-makers want to rely on (while also eliciting forecasts from generalists with strong forecasting track records).

5.   However, some factors make the future potential impact of this work look more promising:
    1.   AI-enhanced forecasting research is a huge factor that will unlock cheaper, faster, high-quality forecasts on any question of one's choosing.
    2.   The next few years of forecasting AI progress/adoption/impact seem critical, and like they'll deliver a lot of answers on whose forecasts we should trust. It seems good to be ready to support decision-makers during this time.
    3.   Leaders in the AI space seem particularly interested in using forecasting in their decision-making; they tend to be both quantitative and open-minded. This creates more potential for forecasting to be useful. More minorly, prediction markets and forecasting are generally becoming more credible within governments.

More detail on some select points below. This comment already got very long (!), so I’ll reserve more elaboration for a future, more comprehensive post.

## **Examples of impact**

Forecasting research has informed some very important decisions. Unfortunately, many of the details of the relevant evidence here cannot be made public. However, there is evidence of substantial public citation of this research, and some public evidence of affecting particular decisions.

A few examples of relevant impact include:
